title: Backtracking
summary: Backtracking is DFS over a tree of *partial solutions*: build a candidate one choice at a time, and the instant it can't lead anywhere, **undo** the last choice and try the next. It powers every "return all subsets / permutations / combinations" question — a Flipkart-OA staple — and with three small templates you can cover 90% of them.

notes.heading: Recognizing a backtracking problem
notes.body:
Before any code, learn the *smell*. A problem is asking for backtracking when:

- It wants you to **enumerate or list all** valid configurations: "return all subsets", "all permutations", "all combinations that sum to X", "all valid placements".
- The answer is a **sequence of choices**, where each step picks from a small menu: include/exclude an element, place a queen in one of $n$ columns, pick the next character.
- Constraints are **tiny** — $n \le 20$ for subsets/permutations, boards around $10 \times 10$. Small $n$ is the setter whispering "exponential search is intended".
- You must **count** or find *one* valid arrangement under combinatorial constraints, and there's no clean greedy or DP formula.

> [!EXAM] Read the constraints first
> $n \le 20$? $2^{20} \approx 10^6$ — a full include/exclude search fits comfortably in the time limit. $n \le 10$ with "all orderings"? $10! \approx 3.6\text{M}$ — permutation search is fine. If instead $n \le 10^5$, backtracking is the *wrong* answer; go look for DP, greedy, or two pointers.

> [!KEY] Keyword tells
> "all possible", "generate", "every way", "return all combinations", "does a path exist". Any of these plus a small $n$ ⇒ start sketching the choice tree.

> [!INTUITION]
> Backtracking is **brute force with a rewind button**. You will still visit an exponential number of candidates in the worst case — the wins come from *never revisiting* dead prefixes (the rewind) and from *pruning* branches you can prove are hopeless.

notes.heading: The universal skeleton: make → recurse → undo
notes.body:
Every backtracking solution is the same recursive function wearing different clothes. You keep one mutable **state** (usually a `vector<int>& cur` holding the choices so far), and `backtrack(state)` does exactly two things:

1. Check a **base case** — if the partial candidate is a complete solution, record it.
2. Loop over the **choices** available right now, and for each one: **make it → recurse → undo it**.

[[fig:m09-make-recurse-undo]]

The *undo* step is the entire trick. After the recursive call returns, you restore the state to exactly what it was before the call, so the next iteration of the loop starts clean. That's what lets one shared `cur` vector safely represent every node of an exponential tree.

> [!ANALOGY] A maze with breadcrumbs
> You walk into a corridor (make a choice), explore everything reachable from it (recurse), and when it dead-ends you walk back to the junction and pick up your breadcrumb (undo) before trying the next corridor. You never teleport — you always retrace your last step. That's why every mutation needs a matching un-mutation.

> [!KEY] Four things define any backtracking problem
> **State** (what you've built so far), **choices** (what you can append next), **base case** (when to record an answer), **prune** (when to give up early). If you can name all four out loud, the code writes itself.

> [!PITFALL] The #1 backtracking bug
> A missing `pop_back()` (or `used[i] = false`, or board un-mark). One forgotten undo doesn't crash — it silently corrupts **every subsequent branch**, and the output is garbage in a way that's painful to debug under a timer. Write the make/undo pair *together*, then insert the recursive call between them.

notes.heading: Templates 1 & 2: subsets and combinations
Template 3: permutations
Handling duplicates: sort, then skip equal siblings
notes.body:
Subsets II and Combination Sum II hand you an array **with duplicates** and demand no duplicate answers. The pattern: **sort first** so equal values sit next to each other, then inside the loop skip a value that equals its predecessor *at the same tree depth*:

```cpp
sort(nums.begin(), nums.end());
for (int i = start; i < n; ++i) {
    if (i > start && nums[i] == nums[i-1]) continue; // skip dup siblings
    ...
}
```

[[fig:m09-dedup]]

Why it works: two loop iterations at the same call (same `start`, same depth) that pick equal values would spawn **identical subtrees** — same remaining choices, same value chosen. Exploring the first is enough; the guard kills the clones.

> [!PITFALL] `i > start`, not `i > 0`
> Comparing against `0` also blocks *parent-then-child* repeats — e.g. it would forbid `[2,2]` entirely, dropping valid answers. You only want to block *sibling* repeats: same value tried twice **in the same loop**. `i > start` says "there was an earlier iteration of *this* loop", which is exactly the sibling condition.

> [!INTUITION]
> Vertical repetition (a duplicate deeper in the tree, under a different parent choice) builds a **different** multiset prefix — keep it. Horizontal repetition (a duplicate tried again by the same loop) rebuilds the **same** subtree — skip it. The dedup guard is a horizontal filter only.

Permutations II needs a subtler rule (`!used[i-1]`) because there's no `start` index — that one gets a full deep dive below.

notes.heading: Pruning, pitfalls, and the interview script
notes.body:
Backtracking without pruning is often TLE with extra steps. The habits that separate AC from timeout:

- **Prune early, prune hard.** Sort candidates and `break` the moment a partial sum exceeds the target (everything after is larger too). Return immediately on an invalid cell. In Generate Parentheses, count opens/closes and never generate an invalid prefix at all — pruning *while building* beats generate-then-filter by an exponential factor.
- **Pass big containers by reference.** State like `vector<int>& cur` or `string& path` must be `&`-references that you mutate-then-restore. Copying at every level multiplies an already-exponential runtime by another factor of $n$ (or worse).
- **`res.push_back(cur)` copies — that's what you want.** The shared `cur` keeps changing; recording a *copy* freezes the answer. Never store pointers or iterators into `cur` — they'll dangle the moment you backtrack.
- **Be explicit about where answers live.** Subsets: *every* node is an answer (record before the loop). Permutations / N-Queens: only *full-length* leaves count (record in the base case). Choosing wrong gives you a flood of partial junk or an empty result.

> [!EXAM] Pruning is the AC/TLE line in timed OAs
> Flipkart-style target-sum questions are written so the unpruned search times out. The sorted-candidates + `break` combo (see the Combination Sum deep dive) typically cuts the tree by orders of magnitude for a one-line cost. Make sorting your reflex whenever a sum or bound is involved.

> [!KEY] The narration script
> Say it out loud before coding: "This is *enumerate-all* with small $n$, so backtracking. My **state** is `cur`, my **choices** at each step are ⟨X⟩, my **base case** is ⟨Y⟩, and I'll **prune** when ⟨Z⟩." Then write the make → recurse → undo skeleton first and fill in the choices. Naming the structure up front signals you *recognize the pattern* instead of groping for it — exactly what pattern-recognition OAs and interviewers reward.

> [!WARNING]
> Complexity answers to have ready: subsets $O(n \cdot 2^n)$, permutations $O(n \cdot n!)$, Combination Sum roughly $O(N^{T/M})$ nodes (target $T$, smallest candidate $M$), Word Search $O(m \cdot n \cdot 4^L)$. Recursion depth (= extra space) is the length of the longest partial candidate.

problems.name: Subsets
problems.note: The canonical include/exclude drill — solve it with Template 1, then again with Template 2's `start` loop.

problems.name: Combinations
problems.note: Pick k of n; pure `start`-index practice — record only when `cur.size() == k`.

problems.name: Combination Sum
problems.note: Reuse allowed: recurse with `i`, not `i+1`; sort + `break` when a candidate exceeds the remaining target.

problems.name: Combination Sum II
problems.note: Each number once (`i+1`) plus the `i > start` duplicate-sibling skip.

problems.name: Permutations
problems.note: Order matters: loop from 0 with a `used[]` array (Template 3), and undo *both* mutations.

problems.name: Permutations II
problems.note: Permutations with duplicates — the trickiest dedup rule: skip when `nums[i]==nums[i-1] && !used[i-1]`.

problems.name: Subsets II
problems.note: Subsets + sort-and-skip-dup: the `i > start` guard in its natural habitat.

problems.name: Letter Combinations of a Phone Number
problems.note: Branch per digit over its mapped letters — a clean multi-way choice tree, no dedup needed.

problems.name: Generate Parentheses
problems.note: Prune with open/close counts while building — never generate an invalid prefix, don't filter afterwards.

problems.name: Palindrome Partitioning
problems.note: The choice is *where to cut next*: extend the current piece only while it's a palindrome.

problems.name: Word Search
problems.note: Grid DFS — mark the cell in place with `'#'`, recurse 4 ways, restore the char on the way out.

problems.name: N-Queens
problems.note: One queen per row; O(1) safety via three sets keyed by `col`, `row+col`, `row-col+n`.

problems.name: Sudoku Solver
problems.note: Constraint propagation on a 9×9 grid — try digits 1–9 per empty cell, undo on failure.

problems.name: Word Search II
problems.note: Backtracking + Trie: walk the grid and the trie together so one DFS prunes many words at once.

deepdives.q: **Combination Sum** — given *distinct* candidates and a target, return all combinations that sum to the target, where each candidate may be used an unlimited number of times. (🎯 OA-critical.)
**Permutations II** — return all *unique* permutations of an array that may contain duplicates, without generating repeats.
**Word Search** — given an m×n grid of characters and a word, determine if the word can be traced through 4-directionally adjacent cells without reusing a cell.
**N-Queens** — place n queens on an n×n board so that no two attack each other (no shared row, column, or diagonal); return all arrangements / count them.

quiz.q: A problem says: *"Given a string s (1 ≤ s.length ≤ 16), return **all** ways to split it into palindromic substrings."* Which approach fits, and what's the giveaway?
options: DP over prefixes that *counts* the partitions — the palindrome structure demands a table
Backtracking over cut positions — "return all" plus a length cap of 16 signals an intended exponential enumeration
Greedy: always extend the longest palindrome from the current position
BFS over states (index, last cut) to find the shortest partition first
quiz.explanation: "Return **all**" means you must materialize every configuration — a counting DP can't list them, and greedy commits to one. Length ≤ 16 means up to $2^{15}$ cut patterns, which is exactly the exponential budget backtracking expects. If instead it asked for the *count* or the *minimum* cuts with length up to $10^5$, then DP would be the right call — constraints choose the tool.

quiz.q: In Template 1 (subsets), you delete the `cur.pop_back()` line after the first recursive call. What actually happens?
options: Compile error — `cur` is a reference and must be restored before reuse
Infinite recursion, since the base case is never reached
Later branches inherit leftover elements, so the output contains oversized, wrong subsets
Only the full set `nums` is returned once
quiz.explanation: Nothing crashes and recursion still terminates (`i` still increments toward `n`) — that's what makes this bug nasty. But `cur` never sheds elements it should have shed, so every "skip" branch after a "take" branch records a polluted vector. One missing undo silently corrupts *all subsequent* branches — the #1 backtracking bug. The compiler can't help you (C++ happily lets you not restore state), which kills option A.

quiz.q: In the Combination Sum DFS, the recursive call is `dfs(i, target - c[i], ...)` — using `i` rather than `i + 1`. What does that change?
options: The same candidate may be chosen again on the next level — reuse is allowed
It generates duplicate combinations like [2,3] and [3,2]
It always causes infinite recursion since `i` never advances
It skips every other candidate, halving the search
quiz.explanation: `i` means "I may pick this candidate one more time"; `i+1` means "move on, never again." Duplicates like [2,3] vs [3,2] are prevented by the `start` mechanism itself (you only ever look forward), not by the `i`/`i+1` choice — so B is wrong. And recursion still terminates because `target` strictly decreases with every pick and the `c[i] > target` check (or `target == 0` base case) stops descent — so C is wrong too.

quiz.q: What is the tight time complexity of generating all subsets of an n-element array, *including* the cost of copying each subset into the result?
options: $O(2^n)$ — one unit per subset
$O(n!)$ — it's a full search tree
$O(n^2)$ — each element pairs with each other
$O(n \cdot 2^n)$ — $2^n$ subsets, each costing up to $O(n)$ to copy
quiz.explanation: There are $2^n$ subsets and `res.push_back(cur)` copies a vector of length up to $n$, so the copying alone is $O(n \cdot 2^n)$ — that factor of $n$ is the part people forget when they answer $O(2^n)$. $n!$ is permutations, a different tree shape entirely.

quiz.q: Run Template 1 (take-branch first, then skip-branch) on `nums = [1,2]`. In what order are subsets appended to `res`?
quiz.explanation: The take-branch recurses first, so the search dives all the way down the all-take path before any undo: take 1 → take 2 → record **[1,2]**; undo 2 → record **[1]**; undo 1, skip 1 → take 2 → record **[2]**; undo 2 → record **[]**. Option A is the order you'd get from Template 2 (record-then-loop) — knowing which template produces which order is a great self-check.

quiz.q: In Permutations II, the dedup guard is `if (i > 0 && nums[i] == nums[i-1] && !used[i-1]) continue;`. Why `!used[i-1]` rather than `used[i-1]`?
options: An *unused* identical predecessor means it was already tried and undone at this same depth, so picking `nums[i]` would rebuild an identical sibling branch
It prevents reading `used[i-1]` out of bounds when i = 0
`used[i-1]` being true would mean the array wasn't sorted, so the guard re-checks sortedness
Either condition works identically; the choice is pure style
quiz.explanation: `!used[i-1]` = "my twin is free at this level" = it was explored and backtracked by an earlier iteration of *this same loop* — choosing `nums[i]` now clones that subtree, so skip. When `used[i-1]` is true, the twin sits in the *current prefix* (a different position), so placing `nums[i]` is genuinely new. B is handled by `i > 0`, not by the flag; and D is half-true in a trap-like way: flipping the condition still dedups, but it prunes much deeper in the tree, so the two are *not* identical in cost.

quiz.q: In N-Queens with diagonal sets, queens stand at (row 2, col 3) and (row 4, col 1). Do they attack each other?
options: No — different rows, different columns means safe
Yes — same column after the `+n` shift
Yes — `row + col` is 5 for both, so they share a ↙ anti-diagonal
Yes — `row - col` is equal for both, so they share a ↘ diagonal
quiz.explanation: Compute both keys: sums are 2+3 = 5 and 4+1 = 5 — equal, so they sit on the same `row+col` anti-diagonal and attack. Differences are 2−3 = −1 and 4−1 = 3 — not equal, so D is wrong. A is the classic oversight: rows and columns clear does *not* mean safe; diagonals are exactly why the `row+col` / `row−col` encoding exists.

m09-make-recurse-undo.caption: The three-beat cycle inside every backtracking loop iteration: **make** the choice, **recurse**, **undo** the choice — then the loop moves to the next choice with the state exactly as it found it.
m09-make-recurse-undo.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 660 175" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Make, recurse, undo cycle of backtracking"><text x="20" y="24" class="t-bold">one loop iteration = make → recurse → undo</text><rect x="30" y="58" width="170" height="54" rx="8" class="f-accent-soft s-accent"/><text x="115" y="80" text-anchor="middle" class="t-accent">1. make choice</text><text x="115" y="100" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-mono">cur.push_back(x)</text><line x1="202" y1="85" x2="240" y2="85" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/><rect x="246" y="58" width="170" height="54" rx="8" class="f-panel s-line"/><text x="331" y="80" text-anchor="middle">2. recurse</text><text x="331" y="100" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-mono">backtrack(...)</text><line x1="418" y1="85" x2="456" y2="85" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/><rect x="462" y="58" width="170" height="54" rx="8" class="f-bad-soft s-bad"/><text x="547" y="80" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bad">3. undo</text><text x="547" y="100" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-mono">cur.pop_back()</text><path d="M 547 112 L 547 150 L 115 150 L 115 120" class="s-muted f-none" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="5 4" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/><text x="331" y="140" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">next choice in the loop, state restored</text></svg>

m09-start-index.caption: The `start` index: the loop only considers candidates from `start` onward (highlighted), so every combination is built in one canonical order. Recursing with `i+1` forbids reuse; recursing with `i` allows it.
m09-start-index.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 660 215" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Start index over a sorted candidate array with reuse rules"><text x="20" y="24" class="t-bold">the start index only looks forward</text><rect x="20" y="50" width="44" height="44" class="f-panel s-line o-60"/><text x="42" y="78" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono t-muted">2</text><rect x="66" y="50" width="44" height="44" class="f-accent-soft s-accent"/><text x="88" y="78" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono t-accent">3</text><rect x="112" y="50" width="44" height="44" class="f-accent-soft s-accent"/><text x="134" y="78" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono t-accent">6</text><rect x="158" y="50" width="44" height="44" class="f-accent-soft s-accent"/><text x="180" y="78" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono t-accent">7</text><text x="42" y="112" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">0</text><text x="88" y="112" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">1</text><text x="134" y="112" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">2</text><text x="180" y="112" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">3</text><line x1="88" y1="162" x2="88" y2="126" class="s-accent" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent)"/><text x="88" y="184" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-accent">start = 1</text><text x="250" y="64" class="t-sm t-muted">index 0 is behind start — never revisited,</text><text x="250" y="84" class="t-sm t-muted">so [2,3] and [3,2] can't both appear</text><text x="250" y="128" class="t-sm"><tspan class="t-mono t-accent">dfs(i+1)</tspan> — no reuse (Combinations)</text><text x="250" y="156" class="t-sm"><tspan class="t-mono t-accent2">dfs(i)</tspan> — reuse allowed (Combination Sum)</text><text x="250" y="184" class="t-sm">loop from 0 + <tspan class="t-mono">used[]</tspan> — order matters (Permutations)</text></svg>

m09-dedup.caption: Duplicate handling on sorted `[1,2,2]`: at the same depth and same `start`, the first `2` is explored, the second `2` is a duplicate *sibling* and gets skipped — its subtree would be identical.
m09-dedup.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 660 205" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Sorted array with duplicate sibling skipped by the dedup guard"><text x="20" y="24" class="t-bold">sorted [1,2,2]: skip the duplicate sibling</text><rect x="20" y="52" width="44" height="44" class="f-panel s-line o-60"/><text x="42" y="80" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono t-muted">1</text><rect x="66" y="52" width="44" height="44" class="f-ok-soft s-ok"/><text x="88" y="80" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono t-ok">2</text><rect x="112" y="52" width="44" height="44" class="f-bad-soft s-bad"/><text x="134" y="80" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono t-bad">2</text><text x="42" y="114" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">0</text><text x="88" y="114" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">1</text><text x="134" y="114" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">2</text><text x="88" y="146" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-ok">explored</text><text x="134" y="172" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-bad">skipped</text><text x="250" y="66" class="t-sm">i = 1: first 2 at this depth → explore</text><text x="250" y="94" class="t-sm">i = 2: nums[2] == nums[1], i > start → skip</text><text x="250" y="128" class="t-sm t-mono">if (i > start && nums[i]==nums[i-1]) continue;</text><text x="250" y="162" class="t-sm t-muted">same value + same start ⇒ identical subtree</text></svg>

m09-nqueens-diag.caption: Every square the queen **Q** attacks belongs to one of three constant-time indices: its column `c`, its ↙ anti-diagonal `row+col`, or its ↘ diagonal `row−col+n`. Safety = three boolean lookups.
